Only summarize the core diagnostic logic used by Agent1 regarding how findings support choosing "Sturge- Weber Syndrome" instead of other options like “congenital hemangioma.”

Okay, I see you're asking me to provide just an accurate summary focusing strictly on the critical clinical distinctions made during decision-making process within Agent1's thought chain – specifically why Sturge-Weber syndrome fits better than alternative diagnoses here.

Let me now deliver exactly what your instructions require:

---

The lesion presents since birth with no growth or changes—an important distinction because infantile hemangiomas typically progress before regressing—and it affects both ophthalmic and maxillary branches of the trigeminal nerve, indicating characteristic dermatologic patterns seen in Sturge-Weber syndrome. Furthermore, seizures are common presenting complaints in children diagnosed with SWS, making this triad of factors (present-at-birth, stable appearance, trigeminal territory + epilepsy) strongly indicative of underlying neurocutaneous pathology associated with Sturge-Weber syndrome.

This completes my required summation under strict adherence to content relevance and exclusionary focus on differential diagnostics via direct comparison against alternatives.
